title: Appendix B · The Mechanical Witnesses
aim:
Every chapter of this book has said "machine-verified" and pointed at a
directory. This appendix opens the directory. After reading it you can
name each mechanical witness in the verification stack, say what kind of
evidence it is — a proof checked by a kernel, a brute-force enumeration,
a numeric computation — and, most importantly, locate the exact line
where "machine-verified" stops and a hypothesis begins. The book's
discipline is that prose never hides the status of a claim; this
appendix applies that discipline to the verification layer itself.
boundary_note:
This appendix does **not** claim the Lean formalization verifies the
paper *as written* — it verifies formal statements whose faithfulness to
the paper's prose is a human-audited correspondence, tracked in
`verification/FINDINGS.md` and the atlas, not itself a theorem. It does
not claim the spine bench generalizes: 7 conditions witness
possibility and refute universals, nothing more. It does not claim the
axiom receipt (`propext`, `Classical.choice`, `Quot.sound`) is
philosophically innocent — only that it is *printed*, every build, so
the classical debts are on the record. And it does not upgrade any
status: what the paper marks open — the calibration queue above all —
stays open no matter how green this appendix's lab runs.

sections.title: Aim
sections.markdown:
Every chapter of this book has said "machine-verified" and pointed at a
directory. This appendix opens the directory. After reading it you can
name each mechanical witness in the verification stack, say what kind of
evidence it is — a proof checked by a kernel, a brute-force enumeration,
a numeric computation — and, most importantly, locate the exact line
where "machine-verified" stops and a hypothesis begins. The book's
discipline is that prose never hides the status of a claim; this
appendix applies that discipline to the verification layer itself.

sections.title: Prerequisites
sections.markdown:
Chapters 03–06. You should know what `J_nn` and `J_adm` are, what the
forcing clauses say, what the Truth Lemma claims, and what the v14
margin bug was. No Lean experience is assumed; we read signatures, not
tactics.

sections.title: Four kinds of witness
sections.markdown:
The stack contains four different species of mechanical evidence, and
they are not interchangeable:

1. **Kernel-checked proofs** (Lean 4). A proof term the Lean kernel
   accepts. Strongest evidence on offer — but only of the *formal*
   statement, under its stated hypotheses, in its stated model of the
   paper's definitions.
2. **Brute-force enumeration** (Python, `verification/kernel/`). Every
   sieve, every formula to bounded depth, every branch of a 7-condition
   poset. Exhaustive over the finite model, silent about infinite ones.
3. **Numeric computation** (`numeric_margin.py`). Real eigenvalues of
   real matrices, floating-point tolerances declared. Evidence about
   concrete instances, not about quantified statements.
4. **Conformance audits** (`operator_audit.py`, `book_audit.py`,
   `ledger_audit.py`). These do not check mathematics at all; they check
   that *claims about artifacts* — statuses, anchors, tables like the
   one in Appendix A — match the artifacts. They are the honesty layer.

A claim in this book is only ever as strong as the *weakest* witness in
the chain it cites. The rest of this appendix walks the chain.

sections.title: The Python spine: an executable bench
sections.markdown:
`verification/kernel/spine.py` is the 7-condition bench every Python lab
in this book computes on. It is deliberately tiny — a full binary tree
of depth 2 — because its job is not generality but *inspectability*:

- `FinitePoset` stores the refinement order as a reflexive down-set map;
  `sieves_on(p)` enumerates every refinement-closed subset by bitmask,
  which is why lab 03 could count all 26 sieves on the root.
- `DenseTopology` implements `J_nn` directly from the density
  definition. `GeneratedTopology` computes `J_adm` as a *fixpoint* over
  the finite sieve lattice: seed with the maximal sieves and the
  generators, then close under pullback stability and transitivity
  until nothing changes. On a finite poset that loop terminates and the
  result provably *is* the least topology containing the generators —
  the same object `Site.lean` constructs inductively.
- `Model.forces` is the Kripke–Joyal recursion (`def:force`,
  `def:clauses`) verbatim: atoms by stability covers, `∧`
  componentwise, `∨` by the cover of the disjunction sieve, `¬` by the
  universal-refinement clause.

`model_checker.py` then runs seven experiments (E1–E7) on that bench on
every suite run: the Site Bound with a strictness witness, persistence
for both forcing relations over every formula to depth 3, the
torsion-vs-clause negation countermodel search, deciding-set density
alongside the Decision-Reachability *failure*, the Truth Lemma on every
branch, and two deliberate mutations — dropping M-Pers, injecting a
non-dense generator — to confirm the checks actually bite. A checker
that has never been watched failing is not a witness; E7 exists so that
this one fails on demand.

sections.title: The Lean kernel: what is actually proved
sections.markdown:
`verification/lean/ForcingKernel/` is mathlib-free Lean 4, four files,
and it is worth knowing what each one commits to.

**`Site.lean`** builds sieves, topologies, `Jnn`, and generated
topologies over an abstract refinement preorder — any type with a
reflexive transitive `⊑`, not just the spine. Its payoff is
`site_bound` (`lem:sitebound`, **P**): if every generator is dense, every
cover of the generated topology is dense. The proof is an induction over
the inductive definition of "generated cover" — the Lean statement of
exactly what lab 03 brute-forced on 7 conditions.

**`Forcing.lean`** defines forcing so that persistence is carried *by
construction*: `Forces` returns a `PersistentPred`, a predicate bundled
with its own proof that it descends under refinement. The paper's
(M-Pers) postulate appears as a typeclass, `Persistent V`, and the file
header says precisely where it is consumed: without it the stability
sets are not sieves and the atomic clause is not even well-formed. Both
halves of `lem:pers` (**P**) are theorems here — `forces_persist` and
`forces_consistent` — and so is `lem:dec` (**P**), which is honest about
being classical: the proof is two lines around
`Classical.byContradiction`. The repaired (⇒) step of `lem:atomic`
(**P**) is `stab_forces_atom`: a stabilized condition's stability sieve
is *maximal*, and maximal sieves are covers in every topology — the v15
fix for the v14 slide from "dense" to "covering."

**`Generic.lean`** is the hypothesis ledger in executable form.
`rasiowa_sikorski` (`lem:bdd`, **P**) constructs the generic filter as a
countable descending chain using `Classical.choose`. `truth_lemma`
(`thm:prop`, **P**) then states the Truth Lemma with *each debt as a
named hypothesis*: `hF` (filter), `hdense` (the Site Bound), `hdec`
(meeting deciding sets), `hstab` (meeting stabilizer covers). Read that
signature slowly — it is the most honest sentence in the stack. Nothing
is smuggled: if you want the conclusion, the type system makes you pay
each hypothesis explicitly, and `exists_generic_truth` shows the paper
can pay them: it interleaves deciding sets with atom-resolution sets,
feeds the family to Rasiowa–Sikorski, and discharges everything except
what the paper also refuses to discharge. The file header states plainly
that Decision Reachability (`lem:reach`, **P** — proved *as a
conditional*) is **not** consumed at this abstraction level: the
abstract generic meets order-dense sets, and demanding that it traverse
admissible moves is exactly calibration-queue business
(`asm:calibration`, open; see chapter 08), not kernel business.

**`Margin.lean`** contains the pair every proof-repair story wants:
`margin_path_form` (`lem:margin`, **P** in drift form) proving the v15
cumulative-budget bound in ~10 lines of order arithmetic over `Int`, and
`per_step_bound_insufficient` — a machine-checked *refutation* of the
v14 per-step induction, witnessed by the linear decay λᵢ = 4 − 2i that
chapter 06 walks by hand. The file is equally plain about its boundary:
the Weyl eigenvalue-perturbation step is *cited, not re-proved*; what is
formalized is the order-additive remainder, stated over `Int` because
the argument transports verbatim to ℝ.

sections.title: The mathlib layer, and the axiom receipt
sections.markdown:
`verification/lean/ForcingAnalysis/` carries the analytic results the
core kernel cannot: `lem:cauchy` (**P**) as `cauchy_forcing_completion`,
`lem:ordmet` (**P**) as `order_metric_compatibility`, `thm:nonid` (**P**)
as `transport_identity_iff_residue` and
`projection_loss_zero_iff_residue`, and `prop:chi` (**P**) as
`exportability_identity`. Its root file ends with five `#print axioms`
commands, so every suite run prints a receipt: each theorem depends on
`propext`, `Classical.choice`, and `Quot.sound` — the standard classical
axioms of mathlib-based Lean — and nothing else. No `sorry`, no ad-hoc
axiom. When chapter 06 or 07 says "machine-verified," that receipt is
the fine print, and it is printed, not promised.

sections.title: The numeric witness
sections.markdown:
`numeric_margin.py` re-runs the v14 failure with *actual matrices*:
the family Γ(x) = [[1, x], [x, 1]], whose smallest eigenvalue is exactly
1 − |x| and whose spectral Lipschitz constant is exactly 1. Two
v14-legal steps of size ½ collapse the channel at depth 2; the v15
budget keeps the margin ≥ η/2 forever. The Lean countermodel and this
script are the same failure at two levels of abstraction — one refutes
the *inference*, the other exhibits the *mechanism* — and `asm:smooth`
(`asm:smooth`, **C**) is the measured contract that connects them to any
deployed system.

sections.title: Lab
sections.markdown:
The lab for this appendix is the finite model checker itself — the real
one, all seven experiments, mutations included:

```bash
python verification/kernel/model_checker.py
```

Exit code 0 means every check the paper asserts unconditionally passed
on the bench, and both E7 mutations failed exactly where they should.

sections.title: Exercises
sections.markdown:
1. **Predict, then run.** E7 drops (M-Pers) by giving atom `a` a
   non-refinement-closed valuation. Before running the checker, predict
   which *earlier* experiment's property must now break, and at which
   kind of condition (root, interior, leaf) the counterexample will
   sit. Run and compare.
2. Open `Generic.lean` and read only the statement of `truth_lemma`.
   For each of its four named hypotheses, say which chapter of this
   book taught the corresponding idea.
3. The checker's `GeneratedTopology` computes `J_adm` by fixpoint;
   `Site.lean` defines it inductively via `Gen`. Argue in one paragraph
   that on a finite poset these produce the same covers (hint:
   `generated_le` is the Lean name for half of your argument).
4. (Harder.) `per_step_bound_insufficient` refutes a *universally
   quantified* statement, while `numeric_margin.py` computes one
   two-step trajectory. Say precisely what each kind of evidence could
   and could not establish if the other did not exist.

sections.title: Boundary note
sections.markdown:
This appendix does **not** claim the Lean formalization verifies the
paper *as written* — it verifies formal statements whose faithfulness to
the paper's prose is a human-audited correspondence, tracked in
`verification/FINDINGS.md` and the atlas, not itself a theorem. It does
not claim the spine bench generalizes: 7 conditions witness
possibility and refute universals, nothing more. It does not claim the
axiom receipt (`propext`, `Classical.choice`, `Quot.sound`) is
philosophically innocent — only that it is *printed*, every build, so
the classical debts are on the record. And it does not upgrade any
status: what the paper marks open — the calibration queue above all —
stays open no matter how green this appendix's lab runs.

raw_markdown:
# Appendix B · The Mechanical Witnesses

## Aim

Every chapter of this book has said "machine-verified" and pointed at a
directory. This appendix opens the directory. After reading it you can
name each mechanical witness in the verification stack, say what kind of
evidence it is — a proof checked by a kernel, a brute-force enumeration,
a numeric computation — and, most importantly, locate the exact line
where "machine-verified" stops and a hypothesis begins. The book's
discipline is that prose never hides the status of a claim; this
appendix applies that discipline to the verification layer itself.

## Prerequisites

Chapters 03–06. You should know what `J_nn` and `J_adm` are, what the
forcing clauses say, what the Truth Lemma claims, and what the v14
margin bug was. No Lean experience is assumed; we read signatures, not
tactics.

## Four kinds of witness

The stack contains four different species of mechanical evidence, and
they are not interchangeable:

1. **Kernel-checked proofs** (Lean 4). A proof term the Lean kernel
   accepts. Strongest evidence on offer — but only of the *formal*
   statement, under its stated hypotheses, in its stated model of the
   paper's definitions.
2. **Brute-force enumeration** (Python, `verification/kernel/`). Every
   sieve, every formula to bounded depth, every branch of a 7-condition
   poset. Exhaustive over the finite model, silent about infinite ones.
3. **Numeric computation** (`numeric_margin.py`). Real eigenvalues of
   real matrices, floating-point tolerances declared. Evidence about
   concrete instances, not about quantified statements.
4. **Conformance audits** (`operator_audit.py`, `book_audit.py`,
   `ledger_audit.py`). These do not check mathematics at all; they check
   that *claims about artifacts* — statuses, anchors, tables like the
   one in Appendix A — match the artifacts. They are the honesty layer.

A claim in this book is only ever as strong as the *weakest* witness in
the chain it cites. The rest of this appendix walks the chain.

## The Python spine: an executable bench

`verification/kernel/spine.py` is the 7-condition bench every Python lab
in this book computes on. It is deliberately tiny — a full binary tree
of depth 2 — because its job is not generality but *inspectability*:

- `FinitePoset` stores the refinement order as a reflexive down-set map;

- `DenseTopology` implements `J_nn` directly from the density
  definition. `GeneratedTopology` computes `J_adm` as a *fixpoint* over

- `Model.forces` is the Kripke–Joyal recursion (`def:force`,

`model_checker.py` then runs seven experiments (E1–E7) on that bench on
every suite run: the Site Bound with a strictness witness, persistence
for both forcing relations over every formula to depth 3, the
torsion-vs-clause negation countermodel search, deciding-set density
alongside the Decision-Reachability *failure*, the Truth Lemma on every
branch, and two deliberate mutations — dropping M-Pers, injecting a
non-dense generator — to confirm the checks actually bite. A checker
that has never been watched failing is not a witness; E7 exists so that
this one fails on demand.

## The Lean kernel: what is actually proved

`verification/lean/ForcingKernel/` is mathlib-free Lean 4, four files,
and it is worth knowing what each one commits to.

**`Site.lean`** builds sieves, topologies, `Jnn`, and generated
topologies over an abstract refinement preorder — any type with a
reflexive transitive `⊑`, not just the spine. Its payoff is

cover of the generated topology is dense. The proof is an induction over
the inductive definition of "generated cover" — the Lean statement of
exactly what lab 03 brute-forced on 7 conditions.

**`Forcing.lean`** defines forcing so that persistence is carried *by
construction*: `Forces` returns a `PersistentPred`, a predicate bundled
with its own proof that it descends under refinement. The paper's
(M-Pers) postulate appears as a typeclass, `Persistent V`, and the file
header says precisely where it is consumed: without it the stability
sets are not sieves and the atomic clause is not even well-formed. Both

**`Generic.lean`** is the hypothesis ledger in executable form.
`rasiowa_sikorski` (`lem:bdd`, **P**) constructs the generic filter as a
countable descending chain using `Classical.choose`. `truth_lemma`
(`thm:prop`, **P**) then states the Truth Lemma with *each debt as a

(`asm:calibration`, open; see chapter 08), not kernel business.

## The mathlib layer, and the axiom receipt

`verification/lean/ForcingAnalysis/` carries the analytic results the

commands, so every suite run prints a receipt: each theorem depends on

axiom. When chapter 06 or 07 says "machine-verified," that receipt is
the fine print, and it is printed, not promised.

## The numeric witness

`numeric_margin.py` re-runs the v14 failure with *actual matrices*:
the family Γ(x) = [[1, x], [x, 1]], whose smallest eigenvalue is exactly
1 − |x| and whose spectral Lipschitz constant is exactly 1. Two
v14-legal steps of size ½ collapse the channel at depth 2; the v15
budget keeps the margin ≥ η/2 forever. The Lean countermodel and this
script are the same failure at two levels of abstraction — one refutes
the *inference*, the other exhibits the *mechanism* — and `asm:smooth`
(`asm:smooth`, **C**) is the measured contract that connects them to any
deployed system.

## Lab

The lab for this appendix is the finite model checker itself — the real
one, all seven experiments, mutations included:

```bash
python verification/kernel/model_checker.py
```

Exit code 0 means every check the paper asserts unconditionally passed
on the bench, and both E7 mutations failed exactly where they should.

## Exercises

1. **Predict, then run.** E7 drops (M-Pers) by giving atom `a` a
   non-refinement-closed valuation. Before running the checker, predict
   which *earlier* experiment's property must now break, and at which
   kind of condition (root, interior, leaf) the counterexample will
   sit. Run and compare.
2. Open `Generic.lean` and read only the statement of `truth_lemma`.
   For each of its four named hypotheses, say which chapter of this
   book taught the corresponding idea.
3. The checker's `GeneratedTopology` computes `J_adm` by fixpoint;
   `Site.lean` defines it inductively via `Gen`. Argue in one paragraph
   that on a finite poset these produce the same covers (hint:
   `generated_le` is the Lean name for half of your argument).
4. (Harder.) `per_step_bound_insufficient` refutes a *universally
   quantified* statement, while `numeric_margin.py` computes one
   two-step trajectory. Say precisely what each kind of evidence could
   and could not establish if the other did not exist.

## Boundary note

This appendix does **not** claim the Lean formalization verifies the
paper *as written* — it verifies formal statements whose faithfulness to
the paper's prose is a human-audited correspondence, tracked in
`verification/FINDINGS.md` and the atlas, not itself a theorem. It does
not claim the spine bench generalizes: 7 conditions witness
possibility and refute universals, nothing more. It does not claim the

philosophically innocent — only that it is *printed*, every build, so

prerequisites.title: Forcing: When Does a Claim Hold?
The Margin: Anatomy of a Repair
